S1: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Straight to the point of care Last updated: Sep 27, 2024 Table of Contents Overview 3 Summary 3 Definition 3 Theory 4 Epidemiology 4 Aetiology 4 Pathophysiology 4 Classification 5 Case history 6 Diagnosis 8 Approach 8 History and exam 10 Risk factors 13 Investigations 14 Differentials 17 Criteria 18 Management 20 Approach 20 Treatment algorithm overview 26 Treatment algorithm 27 Emerging 38 Secondary prevention 39 Patient discussions 39 Follow up 40 Monitoring 40 Complications 40 Prognosis 41 Guidelines 42 Diagnostic guidelines 42 Treatment guidelines 42 Online resources 44 References 45 Disclaimer 59 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Overview Summary Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive disease characterised by degeneration of motor neurons with cortical, brainstem, and ventral cord locations.
S2: ALS usually presents as a combination of upper motor neuron and lower motor neuron symptoms and signs, involving initially one segment of the neuroaxis (i.e., cranial, cervical, thoracic, or lumbosacral), and then progressively spreading, typically to contiguous areas.
S3: Typical presentations include limb-onset and bulbar-onset ALS or, less frequently, respiratory-onset ALS.
S4: Generalisation of the symptoms follows in time, without intervals of remission, exacerbation, or stabilisation, resulting in progressive disability and death.
S5: There is no cure for ALS.
S6: The focus of medical care is to provide supportive and palliative interventions, aiming to optimise the patient's quality of life.
S7: Riluzole prolongs survival and should be offered to patients at the time of diagnosis.
S8: Continued discussions with the patient and their family regarding advance directives, as well as methods of respiratory and nutritional support are critical.
S9: Palliative care options should be presented and discussed before they are needed.
S10: Definition Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disorder characterised by progressive muscle weakness that can start in limb, axial, bulbar, or respiratory muscles and then generalises relentlessly, causing progressive disability and ultimately death, usually from respiratory failure.
S11: ALS is the most common form of motor neuron disease (MND), and the term ALS is often used synonymously with the term MND.

S18: 3 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Theory THEORY Epidemiology The Global Burden of Disease Study 2016 estimated the worldwide all-age prevalence of all motor neuron diseases to be 4.5 per 100,000 people, with an increase in age-standardised prevalence of 4.5% over the period 1990 to 2016.
S19: The estimated all-age incidence was 0.78 per 100,000 person-years.
S20: [8] [9] An earlier analysis of ALS incidence per 100,000 population reported values of 2.1 for Europe, 1.8 in the US, and 0.6 in Asia.
S21: [10] The mean age of onset of ALS is about 62 years, with a peak incidence between 60 and 75 years.
S22: [10] Limb- onset ALS occurs in about 70% of patients, and bulbar-onset disease in around 25% of patients.
S23: [11] The prevalence of ALS is consistently reported to be higher in men than in women, with a ratio of 1.25:1.
S24: [9] [12] Some studies have reported that this difference disappears with age.
S25: [13] There are no definite data regarding the distribution of ALS among people of different ethnicities, but a higher incidence in white people than in Asian, African, and Hispanic groups has been suggested.
S26: [10] [14] Aetiology ALS is mostly a sporadic disease without a clear cause.
S27: However, there is an autosomal dominant genetic cause in 10% of people with ALS.
S28: In the 90% of patients without a monogenic cause, a single factor cannot be identified.
S29: A gene-time-environment hypothesis has been put forward, suggesting that genetic susceptibility, age-related cellular damage, and a burden of environmental exposures may combine to trigger ALS.
S30: [1] Pathophysiology ALS is a neurodegenerative disorder characterised by progressive loss of cortical (frontotemporal), bulbar (pons, medulla), and ventral cord motor neurons.
S31: After motor cell death, retrograde axonal degeneration follows, with subsequent denervation and reinnervation in corresponding muscles.
S32: Although the mechanism of disease is unknown, several theories regarding pathophysiology have been proposed.
S34: Excessive extracellular levels of glutamate (excitatory neurotransmitter) result in increased calcium entry in neuronal cells, causing cell dysfunction - mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, and ultimately cell death.
S35: This may occur due to defective activity of glial glutamate transport proteins, with post-translational defects in mRNA for these proteins being reported.
S36: The anti- glutaminergic drug riluzole improves survival in patients with ALS, thus supporting a role for glutamate neurotoxicity.
S38: Aggregation of both wild-type and mutant proteins is a universal pathological feature in sporadic and familial ALS.
S39: [17] Aggregated proteins can result in either gain-of-function pathology due to direct toxicity, or loss-of-function pathology via aggregates sequestering normal proteins and preventing normal function.
S40: For example, aggregation of the DNA- and RNA-binding proteins TDP-43 and FUS into inclusions impairs their normal function, causing changes to transcription and processing of RNA.

S47: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Theory • Oxidative stress.
S48: Superoxide radicals, oxygen, and hydrogen peroxide might induce neuronal damage and ultimately death through activation of the apoptotic pathway or by damaging neuronal mitochondria.
S49: Damaged mitochondria will also worsen oxidative stress.
S51: There are inflammatory components to the pathophysiological mechanism of ALS.
S52: However, the story around inflammation is complicated, as some components of inflammation are toxic in ALS and other components are protective.
S54: Mitochondrial impairment might cause neuronal degeneration and ultimately death through various mechanisms, including oxidative stress, calcium-mediated excitotoxicity, and activation of programmed cell death/apoptosis.
S55: Mitochondrial abnormalities are well documented in patients with sporadic as well as familial ALS.
S57: This might result from accumulation of neurofilament inclusions with subsequent slowing of the neurofilament transport and deficient dynein-dynactin complex, with dysfunction of retrograde axonal transport.
S58: Deficient axonal transport appears to relate to initiation and progression of the disease.
S60: One of the core pathological features of the majority of ALS cases is the presence of TDP-43 (TAR-DNA binding protein 43) aggregates.
S61: TDP-43 is known to have various functions in transcription, pre-mRNA splicing, and translation control.
S62: The finding that other ALS genes are also involved in RNA regulation (e.g., C9orf72 and FUS) emphasises the importance of RNA biology to understanding ALS pathophysiology.
S63: [21] In normal cells, TDP-43 shuttles back and forth between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.
S64: The loss of nuclear TDP-43 induces accumulation of double-stranded DNA breaks, which could impair genome stability.
S65: In addition, aggregates of TDP-43, mutant FUS, and C9orf72 repeat expansions impair shuttling of cargo between the nucleus and the cytoplasm, which can negatively affect cell function.
S66: [17] Classification Motor neuron disease (MND) may be primary (neurodegenerative) or secondary (related to a non- neurodegenerative cause).
S67: Primary motor neuron diseases can be classified by phenotype, site of onset, and genetics.
S68: Patients should be assessed to determine if they have: • Only upper motor neuron (UMN) signs, only lower motor neuron (LMN) signs, or both • Only motor signs, or also non-motor signs such as behavioural or cognitive changes • Bulbar onset, limb onset, or respiratory onset (defined by the location of the first symptom) • Hereditary MND or sporadic MND.
S69: Primary motor neuron diseases Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)[1] • The most common form of motor neuron disease (MND); the name is often used synonymously with the term MND.
S70: • Represents a combination of UMN and LMN findings.
S71: Typical UMN findings include loss of co- ordinated movement, spasticity, muscle spasms, and hyper-reflexia.
S72: LMN symptoms and signs are weakness with atrophy and fasciculations.
S73: • ALS has a progressive, unrelenting course with median survival of 3-5 years.
S74: Primary lateral sclerosis (PLS)[1] [2] [3] • An isolated UMN disorder characterised by progressive weakness with generalised spasticity affecting speech, upper extremities, and lower extremities.

S81: 5 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Theory THEORY • Patients may go on to develop LMN features assessed either clinically or neurophysiologically, so that the disease evolves to 'UMN-dominant ALS', but this is rare after 4 years from diagnosis.
S82: • Survival is usually often greater than 10 years if there is no conversion to ALS.
S83: Progressive muscular atrophy[2] • An isolated LMN disorder characterised by progressive weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations.
S84: • Some patients develop UMN symptoms and signs later during the disease course, converting into 'LMN-dominant ALS'.
S85: Progressive bulbar palsy • MND that remains isolated to the bulbar segment.
S86: • Most patients with just bulbar involvement initially will progress to have limb involvement, converting to 'bulbar-onset ALS'.
S87: • A minority of patients will remain as having progressive bulbar palsy.
S88: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with frontotemporal dementia • Presents as a combination of symptoms and signs suggestive of ALS and frontotemporal dementia.
S89: • Cognitive deficits might include language, executive function, personality, and behaviour.
S90: These features can precede or follow ALS symptoms.
S91: Case history Case history #1 A 60-year-old man presents with right foot drop, which has developed gradually over the last year and progressed to involve more proximal areas in the last 2 months.
S92: The patient reports associated muscle twitching and painful muscle cramps involving the same areas.
S93: The neurological examination reveals bilateral lower-extremity weakness, more severe on the right, with associated spasticity, atrophy of the right foot intrinsic muscles, diffuse fasciculations, and hyper-reflexia, with deep tendon reflexes being brisker on the right lower extremity, and a positive right Babinski's sign.
S95: Several other family members have been diagnosed with ALS (some have died) with a pattern suggesting autosomal dominant disease.
S96: Case history #2 A 65-year-old woman presents with progressive slurred speech with nasal quality, and episodes of choking on liquids, for the last 4-5 months.
S97: Neurological examination reveals facial and tongue weakness; tongue muscle wasting and fasciculations; dysarthria; hypophonic speech; and brisk reflexes throughout (including jaw jerk).
S98: Other presentations ALS may present as upper motor neuron (UMN)-dominant ALS, lower motor neuron (LMN)-dominant ALS, or bulbar-onset ALS.
S99: Patients may present with associated cognitive and behavioural impairment that may precede the onset of motor neuron disease symptoms or may become evident late in the disease course.
S100: Some of these patients have sufficient frontotemporal impairments to meet the criteria for dementia.

S116: 7 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis DIAGNOSIS Approach Any patient with symptoms suggestive of ALS should be referred to a neurologist without delay, and investigations performed with high priority.
S117: [35] [36] [37] Diagnosis is based on the presence of upper motor neuron (UMN) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs, disease progression, and absence of any other explanation for the presentation.
S118: Diagnosis of ALS is primarily based on clinical criteria.
S119: A thorough history and physical examination are central to the diagnostic process, followed by electrodiagnostic testing when required for additional evidence of occult LMN involvement.
S120: History ALS presents as a combination of UMN and LMN symptoms and signs, involving initially any segment of the neuroaxis (i.e., cranial, cervical, thoracic, or lumbosacral) and then progressively spreading typically to contiguous areas, without intervals of remission, exacerbation, or stabilisation.
S121: Patients will report insidious onset of focal weakness in the absence of pain or sensory symptoms, with symptoms worsening over time.
S122: Initial symptoms may include isolated dysarthria, hand weakness, foot drop, gait changes, or shortness of breath.
S123: On direct questioning, they may have noticed fasciculations, atrophy, muscle cramping, and sometimes weight loss.
S124: [37] • Clinical presentation and disease course can be quite variable between patients.
S125: • Asymmetrical limb involvement is the most common presentation (70% of cases).
S126: Typically, weakness then progresses from the first limb affected to involve the ipsilateral limb or the same limb on the contralateral side.
S127: It is uncommon for weakness to progress non-contiguously from one limb to the limb diagonally across from it.
S128: [17] [21] • If the disease initially involves the bulbar muscles (occurring in around 25% of patients), the next affected segment will typically be neck muscles with head drop, or cervical with involvement of one arm; and then the contralateral arm or ipsilateral leg.
S129: Uncommonly, progression of weakness in patients with bulbar onset can initially skip the cervical segment and involve the lumbar segment.
S130: • Some patients may present with initial involvement of respiratory muscles (1% to 3%).
S131: [38] • The time interval between the onset of symptoms affecting one segment of neuroaxis and progression to the next is variable.
S132: For example, patients with severe bulbar symptoms (e.g., dysarthria, dysphagia) might have preserved limb function for months.
S133: • Up to 50% of patients will have concomitant cognitive or behavioural impairments, which can manifest as disinhibition, obsessive behaviours, impaired decision-making, and occasionally memory impairment.
S134: [5] After elucidating symptom progression, it is important to obtain data regarding relevant family history of ALS, as well as other neurodegenerative conditions such as dementia and parkinsonism.
S135: Familial ALS represents about 10% of all ALS cases, the rest being sporadic.
S136: [22] Dementia typically associated with familial ALS is frontotemporal dementia (FTD), which may have been labelled as Pick's disease.
S137: However, patients with FTD are sometimes misclassified as having Alzheimer's dementia, so a family history of Alzheimer's dementia may be relevant.
S138: Similarly, patients with motor neuron disease can sometimes be misclassified as having parkinsonism.
S139: Physical examination ALS presents with a combination of UMN and LMN symptoms and signs in a given segment of neuroaxis.
S140: These clinical features may involve the limb, axial, bulbar, and/or respiratory muscles.

S147: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis UMN signs consist of: • Weakness in a pyramidal distribution • Spasticity • Hyper-reflexia and other pathological reflexes (usually found on examination): Babinski's sign, Hoffmann's or Trömner's reflexes, crossed adductors (elicited by tapping the finger placed on the medial condyle of the femur or malleolus internus triggering contraction of the adductors of the thigh), exaggerated jaw jerk (elicited by percussion of the finger placed on the chin, with the patient's jaw relaxed and slightly opened), and re-emergence of primitive reflexes such as the palmomental reflex or the snout reflex.
S148: LMN signs include: • Weakness • Atrophy • Fasciculations.
S149: The patient might present with limb and/or axial weakness, with associated hyper-reflexia in the affected segments of the neuroaxis.
S150: Alternatively, the patient's presentation might be with respiratory dysfunction (i.e., dyspnoea or orthopnoea) and bulbar signs such as dysphagia, dysarthria, sialorrhoea, and pseudobulbar affect (inability to control emotions, with uncontrolled crying and/or laughing).
S151: The dysarthria associated with ALS typically has mixed spastic and flaccid components.
S152: Symptoms of frontotemporal dementia may precede a diagnosis of ALS, or may occur during the disease course.
S153: Testing to exclude alternate diagnoses that mimic ALS Given that ALS is a clinical diagnosis, there are two reasons to perform laboratory and/or radiology evaluations.
S154: The first reason is to exclude alternate diagnoses that may resemble ALS, including neuropathies that affect only motor nerves or diseases that cause a combination of LMN and UMN signs due to anatomical compression of spinal cord and nerve roots.
S155: • Neuroimaging is performed primarily to rule out alternative causes for the signs and symptoms.
S156: Imaging the most affected portion of the neuroaxis is indicated in all cases.
S157: [35] • Nerve conduction studies are performed to evaluate the possibility of peripheral nerve disease mimicking ALS, such as multifocal motor neuropathy.
S158: The presence of severe slowing of motor conduction velocity or the finding of conduction block in motor nerves indicates the presence of an alternative aetiology and eliminates ALS as the diagnosis.
S159: [39] • If the nerve conduction studies suggest possible peripheral nerve disease, blood tests to assess vitamin B₁₂ level and the presence of specific antibodies (anti-GM1, acetylcholine receptor, muscle- specific tyrosine kinase, and voltage-gated calcium-channel antibodies) may be indicated.
S160: • In patients without clear bulbar signs, a combination of spinal cord and multiple spinal root compression may resemble ALS; magnetic resonance imaging is performed to rule out this possibility.
S161: • Very rarely, infectious diseases may resemble ALS; lumbar puncture is occasionally performed to rule out such diseases.
S162: Cerebrospinal fluid analysis, if considered, should include routine studies with cytology.
S163: • HIV testing may be considered if there is a history of exposure, and para-neoplastic antibody panels can be added if there is a history of malignancy.
S164: • Creatine kinase may be elevated to a maximum of 1000 units/L as a consequence of denervated muscles in ALS.
S165: Higher levels suggest an alternate diagnosis.

S172: 9 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis DIAGNOSIS Testing to assess LMN involvement in asymptomatic limbs The second role of laboratory evaluation in patients with suspected ALS is to detect the presence of LMN disease in limbs that are clinically unaffected.
S173: [21] [40] [41] • Electromyography (EMG) is performed for this purpose.
S174: Testing may be limited to the study of limbs in which clear symptoms or signs of LMN disease cannot be obtained.
S175: EMG of clinically unaffected limbs or muscles may demonstrate LMN disease and be diagnostic in patients whose clinical presentation is limited to one or two limbs.
S176: • Abnormalities sought include the presence of fibrillation or fasciculation potentials, as well as enlarged, prolonged, and polyphasic motor units that indicate chronic axon loss with subsequent compensatory re-innervation.
S177: • If initial results are equivocal, additional electrodiagnostic studies may include repetitive nerve stimulation and single-fibre EMG.
S178: • The sensory nerve conduction studies should be normal.
S179: Genetic testing Guidelines recommend that all patients with ALS are offered genetic testing with an ALS gene panel that includes the C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, and TARDBP genes.
S180: Additional genetic testing is recommended for genes strongly and definitively associated with ALS as determined by ClinGen, and any gene for which there is an approved gene-targeted therapy.
S181: [24] Genetic counselling and education should be provided for all patients with ALS, and this should precede the offer of testing.
S182: Pre-test counselling should cover the range of possible testing outcomes, and prepare patients for possible personal, psychological, and economical impacts of testing on themselves and family members.
S183: Post-test counselling should also be provided, giving the patients the opportunity to discuss their result and understand the implications for them and their family.
S184: [24] History and exam Key diagnostic factors presence of risk factors (common) • Risk factors include genetic predisposition or family history, and age >40 years.
S185: upper extremity weakness (common) • Results in difficulties performing activities of daily living, such as brushing teeth, dressing, and brushing hair.
S186: Can be the result of upper motor neuron (UMN) or lower motor neuron (LMN) weakness.
S187: stiffness, with poor co-ordination and balance (common) • Weakness as an UMN symptom is usually moderate, with progressive disability resulting from associated stiffness, poor co-ordination, and balance problems.
S188: spastic, unsteady gait (common) • May indicate UMN weakness affecting lower limbs.

S195: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis painful muscle spasms (common) • LMN symptom.
S196: difficulties in arising from chairs and climbing stairs (common) • Caused by LMN weakness affecting proximal lower limbs.
S197: foot drop (common) • Caused by LMN weakness affecting distal lower limbs; associated with tendency to trip and fall.
S198: stiffness and decreased balance with impact on gait (common) • Caused by UMN-type truncal weakness.
S199: head drop (common) • Caused by neck extensor weakness; result of LMN-type axial weakness.
S200: progressive difficulties in maintaining erect posture, with stooping (common) • Caused by paraspinal weakness; result of LMN-type axial weakness.
S202: increased lumbar lordosis and tendency for abdominal protuberance (common) • Caused by abdominal and paraspinal weakness; result of LMN-type axial weakness.
S204: • Hyper-reflexia in an otherwise weak, atrophic limb is suggestive of motor neuron disease pathology.
S205: In a plegic, severely atrophic limb, the presence of even a trace of reflex is considered pathological.
S206: • Pathological reflexes, such as Babinski's sign, positive Hoffmann's or Trömner's reflexes, crossed adductors, or exaggerated jaw jerk can be found.
S207: Re-emergence of primitive reflexes such as the snout and palmomental reflexes may also be observed.
S208: • An equivocal/mute Babinski's sign is considered positive if the contralateral side is clearly negative.
S209: dyspnoea (common) • Dyspnoea and orthopnoea result from progressive diaphragmatic weakness.
S210: • Orthopnoea causes frequent awakenings through the night, with subsequent lost sleep.

S217: 11 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis DIAGNOSIS coughing and choking on liquids (including secretions) and eventually on food (common) • Dysphagia of UMN type results from lack of co-ordination of the tongue and pharyngeal constrictor muscles.
S218: • Dysphagia of LMN type results from actual weakness of the tongue and pharyngeal muscles.
S219: • In both types, the consequence is dysfunction of the oral and pharyngeal phases of swallowing, with increased risk for aspiration.
S220: • Although severe coughing triggered by aspiration of liquid or food is acutely distressing for patient and carer, it is very unusual for a patient with ALS to choke to death.
S221: • With symptom progression, nutrition deficit and weight loss occurs.
S222: strained, slow speech (common) • Dysarthria of UMN type (i.e., spastic dysarthria) results from incoordination of the tongue, lips, and pharyngeal muscles.
S223: slurred, nasal, and, at times, dysphonic speech (common) • Dysarthria of LMN type results from actual weakness of the tongue, lips, palate, pharyngeal muscles, and, sometimes, vocal cords.
S224: • Resulting speech is poorly articulated (slurred), hypophonic (nasal quality), and, at times, dysphonic (hoarse).
S225: hypophonic speech (common) • Poor respiratory support due to respiratory muscle weakness can cause a hypophonic speech pattern, whereby the voice is quiet and cannot be projected, and frequent breaks for breaths interrupt sentences.
S226: Other diagnostic factors propensity for falls (common) • Can result from UMN or LMN weakness.
S227: sialorrhoea and drooling (common) • Usually result from a combination of facial diplegia with poor lip seal and dysphagia.
S228: inappropriate bursts of crying or laughing (common) • Pseudobulbar affect, otherwise called emotional incontinence.
S229: • Inappropriate bursts of crying or laughing are triggered by stimuli that would not be expected to cause such reaction, and are difficult to stop.
S230: cognitive impairment (uncommon) • Various domains of cognitive functions might be affected in a patient with ALS, including psychomotor speed, language, executive function, and memory.
S231: [42] • Most prominent presentation is that of frontotemporal degeneration.

S238: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis features of frontotemporal dementia (uncommon) • May precede a diagnosis of ALS or may occur during disease course.
S239: Has a gradual onset and progression.
S240: • May present with behavioural dysfunction (including emotional blunting, lack of insight, abnormal social conduct, irritability) and/or cognitive impairment (especially executive dysfunction, decreased word generation) with relative sparing of the memory.
S241: • Presence in ALS may correlate with more rapid disease progression.
S242: [43] [44] Risk factors Strong genetic predisposition or family history • Familial ALS represents about 10% of all ALS cases, the rest being sporadic.
S243: [22] Most cases of hereditary ALS are indistinguishable from sporadic ALS.
S244: • Patterns of inheritance involve predominantly autosomal dominant genetics, although less common autosomal recessive forms also occur.
S245: • The most common cause of familial ALS (30% to 40% of cases) is hexanucleotide expansion in the C9orf72 gene.
S246: Normal numbers of the C9orf72 gene hexanucleotide repeat are less than 30, whereas patients with autosomal dominant ALS can have repeat lengths of 700 to 1600.
S247: [23] Patients with an expansion in C9orf72 can present with ALS, ALS with frontotemporal dementia (FTD), or FTD.
S248: Even within the same family, patients may have different phenotypic presentations.
S249: • Other genes include SOD1 (15% to 20% of familial ALS cases), TARDBP, and FUS.
S250: [21] [24] More recently discovered genes associated with ALS include VCP, SQSTM1, TBK1, KIF5A, TIA1, CHCHD10, and OPTN.
S251: [22] Commercial panels can test for 30 associated ALS genes.
S252: age >40 years • ALS is rare in people under 40 years of age.
S253: [13] Mean age of onset of ALS is about 62 years, with a peak incidence between 60 and 75 years.
S254: [10] Age of onset is associated with rate of disease progression, with disease in younger patients tending to progress more slowly than in older patients.
S255: Weak military service • An increased incidence of ALS has been reported among military veterans, although the quality of evidence is limited.
S256: Exposures to intense exercise, trauma/electrical shock, or toxic agents are thought to be possible risk factors in this group.
S257: [25] [26][27] professional athletic activity • The risk of developing ALS may be higher in professional athletes, such as soccer and American football players, and seems to correlate with the number of years of exposure.
S258: [28] High amounts of lifetime physical activity can slightly increase the risk of developing ALS, particularly in people with a genetic predisposition.

S265: 13 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis DIAGNOSIS cigarette smoking • The risk of developing ALS increases with exposure to tobacco.
S266: There is a positive correlation with cumulative pack-years, duration of exposure, and (possibly) sex (i.e., female smokers might have an increased risk for developing ALS).
S267: [31] [32] agricultural chemical exposure • A positive association between ALS and occupational exposure to pesticides, herbicides, insecticides, fungicides, and fertilisers has been described.
S268: [33] lead exposure • Occupational exposure to lead (total lifetime exposure of 200 hours) was found to correlate with increased risk of ALS.

S277: • Can be normal or might show decreased compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude, with severe axonal loss.
S278: • The presence of severe slowing of motor conduction velocity or the finding of conduction block in motor nerves indicates the presence of an alternative aetiology and eliminates ALS as the diagnosis.
S279: [39] • The sensory nerve conduction studies should be normal.

S299: 15 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis DIAGNOSIS Test Result genetic testing • Guidelines recommend that all patients with ALS are offered genetic testing with an ALS gene panel that includes the C9orf72, SOD1, FUS, and TARDBP genes.
S300: Additional genetic testing is recommended for genes strongly and definitively associated with ALS as determined by ClinGen, and any gene for which there is an approved gene- targeted therapy.
S301: [24] • Genetic counselling and education should be provided for all patients with ALS, and this should precede the offer of testing.
S302: Pre-test counselling should cover the range of possible testing outcomes, and prepare patients for possible personal, psychological, and economical impacts of testing on themselves and family members, Post-test counselling should also be provided, giving the patients the opportunity to discuss their result and understand the implications for them and their family.

S309: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis Differentials Condition Differentiating signs / symptoms Differentiating tests Cervical spondylosis with myelopathy and radiculopathy • Presents with lower motor neuron (LMN) signs at lesion level and upper motor neuron (UMN) signs below the lesion level.
S310: • Usually has associated sensory symptoms and bladder and bowel disturbances.
S311: • MRI cervical spine shows cord compression and multiple spinal root compressions.
S312: Multifocal motor neuropathy • Presents with LMN-only signs, involving one or both upper extremities.
S313: • Might begin with severe weakness in a limb without significant atrophy, with atrophy occurring later in the disease course.
S314: [46] • Electrodiagnostic studies: multifocal nerve conduction block (with other locations than the usual entrapment sites).
S315: High GM1 ganglioside antibody titre (up to 80% of patients).
S317: • Weakness affecting mainly the finger flexors and knee extensors.
S320: • Clinical examination should not show UMN signs.
S321: Monomelic amyotrophy • Focal, predominantly LMN signs and symptoms, with usual occurrence in young people.
S323: • Severity of symptoms may progress over a few years while remaining limited to the initially involved limb.
S324: [47] • More frequent in Indian and Japanese populations.
S325: • Clinical evaluation distinguishes this entity from ALS.
S327: • Ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia, extra-ocular muscle dysfunction) are common.
S329: • Can mimic bulbar-onset ALS, if presents with dysphagia, dysarthria, or facial diplegia.
S330: • Weak but otherwise normal tongue in electromyography (EMG).
S331: • Acetylcholine receptor or muscle-specific tyrosine kinase antibodies usually present.
S332: • Repetitive nerve stimulation may be abnormal in both conditions, but in ALS, the EMG of tongue and facial muscles shows ongoing and chronic denervation.

S343: • Occurs in the segments initially involved by polio a long time after the initial viral disease.
S344: [49] • Clinical evaluation distinguishes this entity from ALS.
S345: Primary lateral sclerosis • An isolated UMN disorder.
S346: Slowly progressive weakness with associated spasticity.
S347: • May convert into UMN- dominant ALS if LMN features develop over time.
S348: • Progresses at a slower pace than ALS.
S349: [2] [3] • Serial clinical evaluation provides data for diagnosis.
S351: Progressive muscular atrophy • Isolated LMN disorder.
S353: • Some patients develop UMN symptoms and signs later during the disease course, converting into LMN- dominant ALS.
S354: [2] • Serial clinical evaluations used for diagnosis.
S355: Progressive supranuclear palsy • Characteristic abnormal eye movements: slowness of vertical saccades, progressing to vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, typically causing difficulty with tasks that involve looking downwards (e.g., reading, eating, walking downstairs).
S356: • Axial-predominant (trunk and neck) symmetric parkinsonism and/or freezing of gait - with a poor response to levodopa.
S357: [50] • Brain MRI typically shows significant midbrain atrophy, resulting in various diagnostic radiographical signs in the midsagittal and axial planes.
S358: [51] Criteria Revised El Escorial criteria for ALS[52][53] These criteria require the presence of upper motor neuron (UMN) signs (by clinical evaluation) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs (by clinical, electrodiagnostic, or neuropathological evaluation) involving the cranial, cervical, thoracic, and lumbosacral segments of neuroaxis, with progressive quality over time.

S365: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Diagnosis criteria are mainly used to ensure a homogeneous population in clinical trials and are not particularly helpful in routine clinical practice.
S366: Clinically definite ALS: • Clinical evidence for UMN and LMN signs in 3 segments of neuroaxis.
S367: Clinically probable ALS: • Clinical evidence of UMN and LMN signs in at least 2 segments of neuroaxis • Some of the UMN signs located rostral to the LMN signs.
S368: Clinically probable-laboratory supported ALS: • Clinical signs of UMN and LMN are present in 1 segment • Or UMN signs are present in 1 region and LMN signs defined by electromyography criteria (acute denervation with re-innervation) are present in at least 2 regions, and other diagnoses were excluded.
S369: Clinically possible ALS - criteria needed for clinically probable-laboratory supported ALS could not be met, and: • Clinical signs of UMN and LMN are present in 1 segment • Or only UMN signs are found in 2 or more regions • Or LMN signs are found rostral to UMN signs, and other diagnoses were excluded.
S370: Clinically suspected ALS: • Only clinical signs of LMN or of UMN in 1 or more regions.
S371: Awaji criteria[54] The revised El Escorial criteria were felt to be too restrictive and limited early diagnosis of ALS, thus precluding many patients from involvement in clinical trials.
S372: Therefore, the World Federation of Neurology ALS group proposed a revision to the revised El Escorial criteria, called the Awaji criteria.
S373: The main change was that the Awaji criteria accept electromyography (EMG) fasciculations as evidence of acute denervation on EMG, whereas previously EMG had to demonstrate fibrillation potentials and/or positive sharp waves.
S374: Also, the Awaji criteria state that EMG evidence of denervation is equivalent to clinical bedside evidence, making the 'clinical probable-laboratory supported ALS' category from the revised El Escorial criteria obsolete.
S375: Gold Coast criteria[55] The El Escorial criteria were felt to be overly complicated and prone to error.
S376: In addition, ALS clinicians were concerned that patients would perceive doubt in their diagnosis if they were told that they had 'probable' ALS rather than definite ALS, when there was really no doubt.
S377: Therefore, in 2020 the World Federation of Neurology ALS group proposed new criteria for diagnosis of ALS, called the Gold Coast criteria: 1.
S378: Progressive motor impairment documented by history or repeated clinical assessment, preceded by normal motor function, and 2.
S379: Presence of upper and lower motor neuron dysfunction in at least 1 body region (with upper and lower motor neuron dysfunction noted in the same body region if only 1 body region is involved) or lower motor neuron dysfunction in at least 2 body regions, and 3.

S387: 19 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management MANAGEMENT Approach Currently, there is no cure for ALS, so the focus of medical care is to provide symptomatic management for all patients.
S388: Disease-modifying pharmacological therapies to treat ALS include riluzole, edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate/tauroursodeoxycholic acid.
S389: [11] [56] There is no evidence for a differential response to treatment between patients with familial versus sporadic ALS.
S390: [57] Care of patients with ALS is best provided in a multidisciplinary ALS clinic, with a team comprising a respiratory therapist, a physiotherapist, an occupational therapist a dietician, a speech and swallowing consultant, a social worker, and other specialists as needed.
S391: This approach allows for optimisation of care, and has been shown to be associated with improved survival, fewer hospital admissions, increased use of adaptive equipment, and better quality of life.
S392: [11] [36] [58] [59] [60][61] Immunisations, including pneumococcal vaccination, COVID-19 vaccination, and annual seasonal influenza vaccination, are recommended in patients with ALS, given the underlying chronic pulmonary dysfunction.
S393: Informing patients and their families about the diagnosis, and ongoing counselling regarding prognosis, treatments, and end-of-life issues, with special emphasis on advance directives, are extremely important aspects of the patient-physician relationship.
S394: [11] [35] [36][62] [63] Pharmacological therapy Riluzole All patients are started on riluzole at the time of diagnosis.
S395: [35] [64] [65] Riluzole prolongs survival in patients with ALS, with potential advantage in patients with bulbar onset.
S396: [11] [64][66] Annual survival benefit with riluzole is approximately 9%.
S397: [11] Significant hepatic toxicity and neutropenia are associated with riluzole use, but are rare.
S398: [67] Liver function tests and full blood count should be monitored monthly for the first 3 months, then every 3 months thereafter.
S399: [64] Nausea and lethargy are possible adverse effects of riluzole.
S400: [59] Edaravone Edaravone is a free radical scavenger that exerts neuroprotective effects by reducing oxidative stress in motor neurons.
S401: It is approved in the US and some other countries (but not in Europe) for treating ALS.
S402: Although one phase 3 randomised controlled trial did not show an overall benefit of intravenous edaravone in slowing ALS progression, a benefit has been demonstrated in a subgroup of patients.
S403: [68] [69] The effect of edaravone on survival was not evaluated in the study; one real-world analysis reported that intravenous edaravone was associated with prolonged overall survival in a large predominantly riluzole-treated US cohort.
S404: [70] However, one cohort study reported that although intravenous edaravone therapy for approximately 1 year was feasible and mainly well tolerated, no disease-modifying benefit was observed compared with standard therapy alone (riluzole).
S405: [71] The treatment regimen for edaravone is very intensive, with treatment needed for 14 days initially, followed by treatment for a further 10 consecutive days a month as maintenance therapy.
S406: Edaravone is available in intravenous and oral formulations.

S413: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management Sodium phenylbutyrate/tauroursodeoxycholic acid is a combination of the salt of an aromatic fatty acid and a bile acid.
S414: The mechanism of action in patients with ALS is unclear.
S415: This combination therapy was demonstrated in one clinical trial to modestly slow progression of ALS over a 6-month period in patients with definite ALS (as defined by the revised El Escorial criteria) who had onset of symptoms within the previous 18 months.
S416: The magnitude of effect was similar to the effect size of edaravone over a 6-month period.
S417: [56] A larger confirmatory trial with expanded inclusion criteria is ongoing, with results expected in 2024.
S418: The combination drug received US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval in 2022 and has conditional approval in Canada, but is not yet approved in Europe.
S419: Symptomatic management ALS is a progressive disease of the motor system and, as such, produces symptoms primarily relating to the location and degree of weakness.
S420: The exact treatment needs vary between patients, and will change with time for individual patients.
S421: [11] [35] [36] [59] [65] [72] • Respiratory and nutritional support is critical, with the actual intervention required dependent on the status of the patient.
S422: • Patients with significant weakness of bulbar musculature may require interventions for dysphagia, sialorrhoea, and communication difficulties.
S423: • Patients with weakness in the extremities may require support aimed at maintaining mobility, therapy for spasticity, or devices aimed at maintaining upper extremity function.
S424: • Mental status changes that may require treatment include depression, anxiety, and pseudobulbar affect.
S425: Cognitive and behavioural changes associated with frontotemporal impairment may also require interventions.
S426: General principles of respiratory dysfunction management Patients with mild or moderate respiratory dysfunction may be entirely asymptomatic, or may have symptoms that are either nocturnal or exercise-related.
S427: Monitoring of respiratory function every 3 months is recommended by screening for subjective symptoms of respiratory insufficiency and objective signs of respiratory insufficiency.
S428: Symptoms of respiratory insufficiency may be shortness of breath on exertion or at rest, orthopnoea, frequent awakenings at night, early morning headaches or confusion (due to nocturnal hypercapnia), poor vocal projection, and poor cough efficacy.
S429: Options for objectively monitoring respiratory function include forced vital capacity (FVC), slow vital capacity (SVC), and sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP).
S430: [11] [72] As supine FVC correlates well with symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation, monitoring may be considered, along with erect FVC.
S431: [65] SNIP may be particularly useful in patients with bulbar weakness who might not be able to form a complete seal around the mouthpiece for obtaining FVC.
S432: [73] SNIP <40 cm H₂O has been found to be related to nocturnal hypoxia and has demonstrated a higher sensitivity for predicting 6-month mortality when compared with FVC <50%.
S433: In addition, nocturnal oximetry and/or sleep study can provide data regarding nocturnal hypoxia.
S434: [65] [72] Symptomatic patients or asymptomatic patients with at least one of: FVC values of less than 50% to 65% of predicted value, or SNIP <40 cm H₂O, or abnormal nocturnal oximetry Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) is indicated for these patients.
S435: [35][36] [58] [72] Historical guidelines have used a threshold for asymptomatic patients of FVC <50% of predicted value for initiation of NIV.

S442: 21 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management MANAGEMENT More recent expert-based best practice recommendations have suggested earlier initiation of NIV in asymptomatic patients, with a threshold of FVC <65% of predicted, because of evidence suggesting that NIV initiation at FVC around 50% is better than at FVC below 50%.
S443: [11] [72][74] All guidelines suggest NIV initiation for symptomatic patients, even if they have FVC >65%.
S444: When NIV is prescribed, modification in pressure settings to ensure maximum comfort is often required, as is individual determination of the most appropriate interface (contact between ventilator and patient).
S445: Anxiolytic medicine, such as lorazepam, might help with the process of adjustment to this type of treatment.
S446: NIV is usually used initially at night, but with symptom progression can be used up to 24 hours per day.
S447: Patients usually need up-titration of NIV pressures as their respiratory function declines.
S448: Also, patients often require different interfaces for day use and night use as they increase their NIV usage, to avoid constant pressure points with a single mask and to allow for better integration with activities.
S449: [72] For example, some patients with ALS will still be able to speak if supported by a nasal-only interface for NIV during the day.
S450: Successful NIV use has been shown to have a positive impact on quality of life and survival.
S451: [65] [75] Diaphragm pacing should not be used for patients with ALS because it is ineffective and may be harmful.
S452: [11] [76] [77] Severe respiratory insufficiency not sufficiently treated with NIV For patients who would consider long-term invasive ventilation, tracheostomy and permanent positive pressure ventilation can be life-prolonging.
S453: [65] [72][78] Consideration of treatment should be preceded by revisiting advance directives and a thorough discussion regarding the type of care that is needed for patients with invasive ventilation.
S454: This discussion should take place well in advance of respiratory failure.
S455: [11] [62] [72] As caring for a patient on permanent mechanical ventilation requires a high level of skill and 24-hour support for management of the ventilator and deep suctioning, most often patients cannot be supported in a home environment and placement in a chronic hospital environment is required.
S456: Less than 10% of patients with ALS consider invasive ventilation.
S457: With permanent ventilation in place, patients may survive a variable number of years.
S458: Invasive ventilation can be withdrawn any time at the patient's request, although it is recognised that this may become a difficult decision, particularly if the patient has developed cognitive impairment or has progressed to a locked-in state, with no means left to express their wishes.
S459: [11] [79] For patients who would not consider long-term invasive ventilation and who have progressed to this degree of severity of respiratory dysfunction, care becomes palliative.
S460: [11] [62] Palliative care focuses on comfort, with special emphasis on symptomatic treatment of shortness of breath with opioids and/or benzodiazepines and consideration of concurrent support via NIV.
S461: Increased airflow in the room via an open window or fan can also be considered.
S462: [59] [80] [81] Mild to moderate symptomatic dysphagia with mild weight loss (<10%) For weight loss, the first step is diet modification.
S463: A dietician should be involved to suggest nutritionally adequate substitutions.
S464: Diet modification with high-calorie supplements is usually effective in maintaining weight for a variable period of time, after which other options must be discussed.

S471: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management For management of dysphagia, texture modification of food can be suggested by a speech-language pathologist or occupational therapist trained in dysphagia assessment.
S472: Early intervention may include avoiding dry and particulate food.
S473: As dysphagia worsens, the swallowing assessor may suggest progression to pureed food and thickening of liquids.
S474: Moderate symptomatic dysphagia with significant weight loss (>10%) For patients who are unable to maintain a stable weight, percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) or radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG) tube placement should be suggested.
S475: Feeding tube placement should be discussed as part of the decision-making related to long-term care.
S476: Nutrition by feeding tube allows for weight stabilisation, and overall might have a beneficial impact on survival.
S477: [11] [35] [65][83] It has an uncertain impact on patient quality of life.
S478: [65] [83] [84] Gastrostomy surgery should be considered before FVC decreases below 50% of its predicted value, to reduce the risks of perioperative morbidity associated with respiratory dysfunction, even if the patient does not have significant dysphagia at that time.
S479: [65] [85] If a feeding tube is placed pre-symptomatically, patients are encouraged to maintain the oral intake as tolerated, while the tube is only flushed with normal saline daily to maintain patency.
S480: Even with tube feeding, aspiration precautions should be maintained.
S481: There is insufficient evidence on whether PEG or RIG tube feeding is better in terms of safety and clinical outcomes.
S482: [11] [86] RIG allows placement in patients with more advanced respiratory failure, but RIG tubes have been found to have a high rate of blocking, leaking, and needing to be replaced in the months after insertion.
S483: [86] Dysarthria Most (but not all) patients with ALS experience significant dysarthria progressing to anarthria.
S484: Speech therapy is usually not effective, so the most effective intervention consists of finding appropriate communication strategies to substitute for normal speech.
S485: This may include writing, as well as use of a variety of augmentative and alternative communication systems that can be activated with hand movements, facial movements, eye gaze, or whatever movement a given patient is able to perform.
S486: [11] [59][87][88] As the patient's mobility is progressively changing, close monitoring and regular re- assessment is necessary to enable them to maintain effective communication ability.
S487: Sialorrhoea Patients with dysphagia often experience drooling because of inability to swallow saliva.
S488: Symptomatic treatments usually consist of anticholinergic drugs such as hyoscyamine hydrobromide, amitriptyline, atropine, or glycopyrronium.
S489: [11] [72] For patients with refractory sialorrhoea, botulinum toxin type B may be considered.
S490: [11] [59] [72][89] If injected into each parotid and submandibular gland, it can interrupt saliva release; however, localisation for injections is variable and the response to botulinum toxin injections is also variable.
S491: When effective, repeat injections are usually required at approximately 3-month intervals.
S492: Maximum benefit is usually experienced at 4 weeks.
S493: [90] Low-dose radiotherapy to the salivary glands is also possibly effective in patients with medically refractory sialorrhoea, although evidence is uncertain.
S494: [11] [59] [72][89] It may decrease saliva secretion for up to 6 months.

S501: 23 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management MANAGEMENT Difficulty with mucous secretions Reduced cough volume and strength may result in inability to expel pulmonary secretions.
S502: Mechanical cough assist devices may be very helpful, particularly if peak cough flow is reduced.
S503: [11] [65] [72] Carbocisteine is a mucolytic that can break down mucus, making it easier to clear, but it is not available in the US.
S504: [92] Other mucolytics such as nebulised acetylcysteine or oral guaifenesin can be tried.
S505: [11] Ensuring sufficient hydration and reduction in anticholinergics can potentially help with management of thick secretions.
S506: Occasionally, patients may elect to undergo tracheostomy to allow for more complete suctioning of airways, but this is uncommon.
S507: Pseudobulbar affect Occasionally patients with significant bulbar upper motor neuron signs will also display pseudobulbar affect, manifested by excessive or inappropriate laughing or crying.
S508: Most often, explanation of the aetiology of these symptoms and reassurance are sufficient to allow patients to deal with this symptom.
S509: If treatment is needed, the combination of dextromethorphan and quinidine has been found to be efficacious in controlling the pseudobulbar affect and is well tolerated.
S510: [11] [59] [93] Alternatively, amitriptyline may be considered for treating sleep problems and pseudobulbar affect, or a selective serotonin-reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) may be used to treat depression and pseudobulbar affect.
S511: [11] Physiotherapy for muscle weakness Physiotherapy is for maintenance of muscle elasticity and joint range of motion, to prevent contractures, and to determine the level of appropriate bracing and need for ambulation devices.
S512: Such devices include ankle foot orthoses, cervical collars (neck weakness), walking sticks, walkers, and wheelchairs.
S513: In each case, a range of devices is available, and the choice must be made by an experienced professional, taking into account the patient's needs and preferences.
S514: [61] Functional decline is an inevitable feature of ALS, so serial evaluations by physiotherapists will be necessary throughout the course of the illness.
S515: Individualised exercise programmes of moderate intensity are considered safe and beneficial for patients with early disease.
S516: [11] [94] [95] These should be home-based and monitored by physiotherapists, using telemedicine approaches if necessary.
S517: [61] Occupational therapy for muscle weakness The goal is to assess the needs of a patient in order to support their independence in performing activities of daily living and to assist with the care for patients with advanced disease.
S518: Examples include devices to assist with transfer from and to a wheelchair (e.g., bed rails, Hoyer-lift) and adapting the environment (e.g., ramps, shower chair, commode, powered hospital-type bed, modifying keyboards, adaptive utensils, accessible clothing).
S519: Serial evaluations by occupational therapists will be required as the disease progresses, in order to define the patient's needs at different points in time.
S520: [11] [36] Therapy for fully paralysed patients Treatment goals relate to maintaining skin integrity and comfort.
S521: Motorised beds and water and air mattresses can reduce pressure and vary sites of skin contact, preventing pressure ulcers and aiding in providing adequate pain control.

S528: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management Muscle spasms and spasticity Muscle spasms are usually a reflection of spasticity, and are treated with exercises and antispasticity medicine.
S529: Medications that may be considered include baclofen, tizanidine, botulinum toxin, benzodiazepines, and cannabinoids.
S530: [11] [59][97] [98] Antispasticity medications can sometimes worsen ambulation or transfer ability due to the emergence of underlying muscle weakness when limb tone is reduced, and benzodiazepines bring a risk of respiratory suppression.
S531: Physiotherapy for spasticity aims to reduce muscle tone, maintain range of motion and mobility, and improve comfort.
S532: Modalities include strengthening, stretching, and positioning exercises.
S533: Individualised exercise programmes of moderate intensity are considered safe and beneficial for patients with early disease.
S534: [94] [95] Therapy to actively or passively perform range-of-motion movements will reduce tone as effectively as pharmacological therapy, but is time-consuming and often requires family members or therapists to provide passive movements.
S535: Depression and anxiety Symptoms of depression and anxiety should be sought at every patient visit.
S536: The incidence of depression in patients with ALS is not well studied, but may be higher than in the general population, especially in those with advanced disease.
S537: [99] Depression is often well treated pharmacologically with an SSRI or serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor (SNRI).
S538: [11] [59] Because most patients with ALS eventually develop dysphagia, it is appropriate to consider initiating treatment with an antidepressant that can be safely crushed (by avoiding capsules or coated medications).
S539: Evidence for psychological interventions for patients with ALS is limited, but there is some evidence of effectiveness for mindfulness and cognitive behavioural therapy for treating depression and anxiety.
S540: [100] [101] Insomnia If insomnia is a problem, it is important to determine the cause so that appropriate treatment can be started.
S541: If insomnia is related to respiratory insufficiency, nocturnal NIV may be an effective treatment.
S542: Other possible causes of insomnia include mood disorders and pain.
S543: [11] Treatment of insomnia with benzodiazepines should generally be avoided in patients with ALS due to the risk of respiratory suppression.
S544: Non-benzodiazepine sleep medication such as melatonin or zopiclone can be considered.
S545: Cognitive and behavioural impairment Up to 45% of patients with ALS have associated cognitive and behavioural impairment, and in approximately 14% of patients these deficits are severe enough to meet criteria for frontotemporal dementia.
S546: [102] Symptoms may precede the onset of motor neuron disease symptoms, or may become evident late in the disease course.
S547: [4] [103] Screening for behavioural, cognitive, and language dysfunction should be carried out for patients with ALS.
S548: [11] [59] Interventions are generally supportive, as there is no evidence for effectiveness of pharmacological therapies.
S549: Palliative care ALS does not have a cure, and weakness and functional deficits will progress throughout the disease course.

S622: [65] SNIP may be particularly useful in patients with bulbar weakness who might not be able to form a complete seal around the mouthpiece for obtaining FVC.
S623: [73] SNIP <40 cm H₂O has been found to be related to nocturnal hypoxia and has demonstrated a higher sensitivity for predicting 6-month mortality when compared with FVC <50%.
S624: In addition, nocturnal oximetry and/or sleep study can provide data regarding nocturnal hypoxia.
S625: [65] [72] » NIV is recommended for patients with FVC of less than 50% to 65% of predicted value, or maximal SNIP <40 cm H₂O, or abnormal nocturnal oximetry.
S626: [35] [36] [58][72] Historical guidelines have used a threshold for asymptomatic patients of FVC <50% of predicted for initiation of NIV.
S627: More recent expert-based best practice recommendations have suggested earlier initiation of NIV in asymptomatic patients, with a threshold of FVC <65% of predicted, because of evidence suggesting that NIV initiation at FVC around 50% is better than at FVC below 50%.
S628: [11] [72][74] All guidelines suggest NIV initiation for symptomatic patients, even if they have FVC >65%.
S629: » When NIV is prescribed, modification in pressure settings to ensure maximum comfort is often required, as is individual determination of the most appropriate interface (contact between ventilator and patient).
S630: Anxiolytic medication, such as lorazepam, may help with the process of adjustment to this type of treatment.
S631: » NIV is usually used initially at night, but with symptom progression it can be used up to 24 hours per day.
S632: Patients usually need up-titration of NIV pressures as their respiratory function declines.
S633: Also, patients often require different interfaces for day use and night use as they increase their NIV usage to avoid constant pressure points with a single mask and to allow for better integration with activities.
S634: [72] For example, some patients with ALS will still be able to speak if supported by a nasal-only interface for NIV during the day.
S635: Successful NIV has been shown to have a positive impact on quality of life and survival.
S636: [65] [75] » Chronic invasive ventilation is an option for patients with severe respiratory insufficiency not sufficiently treated with NIV.
S637: For those who would consider long-term invasive ventilation, tracheostomy and permanent positive pressure ventilation can be life-prolonging.

S760: 37 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Management MANAGEMENT Emerging Stem cells There has been substantial interest in the potential of stem cells to treat ALS.
S761: Trials have used different types and methods of delivery of stem cells.
S762: One phase 1/2 trial of implantation of human spinal cord-derived neural stem cells in the ventral horn of both cervical and lumbar spine of patients with ALS showed that injections can be performed safely.
S763: [107] Studies have been carried out on both peripheral and intrathecal injections of treated adult bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) designed to produce a variety of neurotrophic factors.
S764: One phase 3 study using intrathecally administered neurotrophic factor- secreting stem cells failed to reach its primary end point, but there was some improvement noted in biochemical markers.
S765: [108] Expert opinion is mixed as to whether this method and mechanism of stem cell delivery should continue to be developed or abandoned.
S766: One systematic review concluded that there is a lack of high-quality evidence to recommend stem-based therapies and that further trials are needed.
S767: [109] Masitinib Masitinib is an anti-inflammatory agent that targets mast cells and microglia.
S768: One randomised controlled phase 2/3 trial reported slowing of decline of function in patients receiving masitinib in combination with riluzole, compared with placebo plus riluzole.
S769: Adverse events included maculopapular rash and peripheral oedema.
S770: [110] [111] A phase 3 trial is under way.
S771: [112] Ibudilast Ibudilast is an oral, small-molecule phosphodiesterase and a macrophage migration inhibitory factor inhibitor, which suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines and promotes neurotrophic factors.
S772: The pharmacokinetics, safety, tolerability, and efficacy of ibudilast on function, muscle strength, quality of life, and survival in ALS patients are being investigated in a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre phase 2b/3 study.
S773: [113] Tofersen Tofersen (also known as BIIB067) is an investigational antisense oligonucleotide that reduces the levels of the abnormal protein superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) by binding to SOD1 mRNA.
S774: In one phase 1/2 trial, SOD1 concentrations were reduced in cerebrospinal fluid following intrathecal administration of tofersen over 12 weeks in adults with ALS due to SOD1 gene mutations.
S775: [114] Although a placebo-controlled phase 3 trial of intrathecal tofersen did not improve clinical end points, data from the open-label extension phase suggest that patients randomised to the treatment arm had improved outcomes during the open-label phase compared with patients receiving placebo initially.
S776: [115] The potential effects of earlier compared with later initiation of tofersen are being evaluated in an ongoing extension study.
S777: [116] The ATLAS study evaluating early treatment with tofersen in pre-symptomatic patients carrying a SOD1 mutation is under way.
S778: [117] Reldesemtiv Reldesemtiv is an activator of fast skeletal muscle troponin, which may improve the efficiency of muscle contraction in weak muscles.
S779: An international phase 3 study is evaluating whether reldesemtiv may slow the reduction in vital capacity or hand grip in patients with ALS.
S780: [118] AP-101 AP-101 is a human monoclonal antibody designed to selectively target and reduce levels of misfolded SOD1, which can be seen in the pathology of both sporadic ALS and SOD1 hereditary ALS.
S781: This intravenous therapy is currently undergoing a phase 2 trial.

S791: An intravenous formulation of trehalose is being evaluated as part of the Healey ALS platform trial.
S792: [121] Secondary prevention Immunisations, including pneumococcal vaccination, COVID-19 vaccination, and annual seasonal influenza vaccination, are recommended in patients with ALS, given their underlying chronic pulmonary dysfunction.
S793: Patient discussions Communication of the diagnosis and information relating to ALS should be tailored to the needs of the patient and their family.
S794: Not all patients will want detailed information about prognosis straight away.
S795: Patients should be asked about their preferences for involving their family members and/or other carers.
S796: Both verbal and written information about symptoms, management (especially nutritional and respiratory support), and specific treatments should be provided.
S797: [11] [36] [63] [72] All patients with ALS should be offered genetic testing, and both pre-test and post-test counselling.
S798: Pre- test counselling should cover personalised risk assessments and the range of possible outcomes, and prepare the patient for possible personal, psychological, and economical impacts of testing on themselves and family members.
S799: Post-test counselling allows the patient to discuss their result and the implications for them and their family.
S800: [24] Advance directives and wishes for end-of-life care should be discussed with the patient and family/carers as early as possible (but only when they are ready to do so), and long before hospice care is needed.
S801: Ongoing discussion of advanced care planning should be part of regular follow-up.
S802: [11] [36] [62] Patients who have feeding tubes and their carers should be instructed in their use: for example, how to prevent clogging.
S803: [129] The patient, and their family members and other carers, should be advised to seek immediate medical evaluation if the patient shows symptoms of severe respiratory dysfunction, especially when associated with fever.
S804: Online information from recommended sources may be helpful for patients and families.

S811: 39 Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Follow up FOLLOW UP Monitoring Monitoring • Monitoring for respiratory decline (forced vital capacity) is recommended every 3 months, or as clinically indicated.
S812: [11] [36] • Monitoring for nutrition deficit (weight and body mass index) is also recommended every 3 months, or as clinically indicated.
S813: [11] • In patients taking riluzole, monitoring for hepatotoxicity (liver function tests) and neutropenia (complete blood count) should be done every month in the first 3 months and every 3 months afterwards.
S814: [11] [64] [128] • Patients should be asked regularly about pain, mood disorders, and insomnia.
S815: [11] Complications Complications Timeframe Likelihood respiratory failure variable high Respiratory failure is an inevitable consequence of ALS.
S816: Early symptoms can be effectively treated with non-invasive ventilation, while severe end-stage respiratory failure requires either tracheostomy, with permanent assisted ventilation, or palliative care.
S817: nutritional deficit variable medium Every effort should be made to ensure that ALS patients receive adequate nutrition throughout the course of their disease.
S818: aspiration pneumonia variable medium In patients with dysphagia and difficulty with airway maintenance, choking and aspiration pneumonia may develop at any time.
S819: The risk for aspiration pneumonia persists in patients on tube feeds; thus, precautions against aspiration should be maintained after feeding tube placement.
S820: riluzole-related hepatotoxicity variable low There is a risk of hepatotoxicity associated with the use of riluzole; however, with careful monitoring of liver function tests (LFTs), this adverse effect may be prevented.
S821: Drug should be discontinued if there is a persistent increase in LFTs, or with alanine aminotransferase exceeding 5 times the upper limits of normal.
S822: [128] riluzole-related neutropenia variable low There is a rare risk of neutropenia associated with the use of riluzole.
S823: Close monitoring of FBC every month for the first 3 months and every 3 months thereafter is necessary during treatment.
S824: Drug must be discontinued if neutropenia is identified.

S831: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis Follow up Prognosis ALS follows a progressive course without intervals of remissions, relapses, or stabilisation, causing progressive disability and ultimately death.
S832: The disease is highly variable between affected individuals in terms of clinical presentation and time course.
S833: Median survival is 3-5 years, but survival for up to 10 years and even beyond (in approximately 10% to 20% of patients) has been reported.
S834: [21] [122] [123] Prognostic factors Prognostic factors associated with more prolonged survival include: • Treatment with non-invasive ventilation (NIV)[123] • Enteral nutrition[84] • Younger age at diagnosis[124] [125] • Limb onset[124] [126] • Baseline forced vital capacity (FVC) of >75% (median survival 5 years)[126] • Longer time from symptom onset to diagnosis.
S835: [125] Prognostic factors associated with worse outcome include: • Older age at diagnosis • Bulbar onset • Comorbidity with frontotemporal dementia • Baseline FVC of <75% • Substantial weight loss.
S836: [127] The rate of symptom progression is considered to be an independent prognostic factor.
